body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
irrlicht_observes: yes

## Reasoning

Claude Code ships an in-product 'auto mode' (footer 'auto mode on', driver waits for this string before sending input — see drive-claudecode-interactive.sh). In auto mode the runtime's classifier auto-approves safe tool calls (Read, Grep, contained Edit, contained Bash within allow-listed paths) and still gates risky ones (writes to .git/.claude/.vscode/.idea/.husky, network-egress shells, broad bash patterns) through the normal permission prompt. So the agent_supports answer is unambiguous yes — the feature exists and ships in current TUI builds.

irrlicht observes the two paths via independent transports and they collapse onto exactly the two state arcs the spec demands.

**Safe (auto-approved) tool call — `ready → working → ready`, never `waiting`.** A safe tool_use appears in the JSONL transcript, the tailer marks `HasOpenToolCall=true`, ClassifyState returns `working` (rule 4). When the tool_result arrives in the same transcript, `HasOpenToolCall` goes back to false and the eventual `stop_reason=end_turn` (or `system/turn_duration` event mapped to `turn_done`) lets `IsAgentDone()` fire → `working → ready` (rule 2b). Crucially Claude Code does NOT fire a PermissionRequest hook for an auto-approved call (hooks.go HookPermissionRequest only dispatches when the runtime actually shows the prompt), so `PermissionPending` never gets set and rule 0 never trips. The waiting state cannot appear.

**Risky tool call — `working → waiting`, then resume.** When the classifier decides the call needs user approval, Claude Code fires the PermissionRequest hook ahead of (or alongside) the tool_use JSONL event. The hook receiver in `hooks.go` dispatches into `SessionDetector.HandlePermissionHook`, which sets `state.Metrics.PermissionPending = true` (session_detector_activity.go:346). On the next classifier pass rule 0 transitions to `waiting` (state_classifier.go:32). When the user approves, Claude Code fires PostToolUse (or PostToolUseFailure on deny — observably identical to irrlicht); the receiver clears the flag, classifier returns to `working`, and the eventual turn_done returns to `ready`. This is exactly the contract the spec asserts.

## What irrlicht observes about the classifier itself

Nothing — and intentionally. irrlicht does not inspect Claude Code's classifier decision; it only sees the downstream observable (hook fired vs not). That's the correct boundary: the classifier's verdict is upstream policy. The spec also only asserts the downstream state arcs, so the assessment is irrlicht_observes=yes with no transport gap.

## Caveats

The hook installer (hookinstaller.go) and statusline installer must be in place for the hook path to wire up — that's part of the daemon's first-run setup and not scenario-specific. The recipe must drive Claude Code in auto mode (the driver script already handles that). The recipe should mix at least one safe + one risky call to exercise both branches in the same session.
caveats: Requires Claude Code in 'auto mode' (driver script sends `<shift>+<tab>` and waits for `auto mode on` in the footer); a recipe that forgets to enable auto mode would exercise the normal permission-prompt path instead and the safe-call assertion (never enters waiting) would no longer be meaningful.

The risky call must target a path or command the classifier actually gates. Future tightening or loosening of Claude Code's allow-list could turn a previously-risky shell into an auto-approved one and silently flip the second arc to never-waiting. The recipe should pick a stable gate trigger (e.g. write under `.git/`).

The Stage-1 spec name maps to 'Auto-classified permission approval' (March 2026 Claude Code Auto Mode). Older Claude Code releases without auto mode would map to a different cell (Tool gate via permission prompt) — agents below 2.1.112 may not surface the auto-classified path at all.

sources.note: PermissionRequest/PostToolUse hook handler: PermissionRequest dispatches when Claude Code actually shows a prompt (i.e. classifier did NOT auto-approve); auto-approved calls bypass this path entirely.

Rule 0 (`PermissionPending → waiting`) is the only path that can flip a session to waiting from the hook. Without a PermissionRequest the rule never fires and the session stays on the rule-4 → rule-2b working→ready arc.

Line 346: `state.Metrics.PermissionPending = true` is set only via the hook receiver — there is no parser-side path to this flag, which is why auto-approved JSONL tool_use events cannot accidentally flip it.
Driver supports `auto mode on` footer detection — the recipe primitive needed to put Claude Code into the classifier-driven mode for this scenario.
